questions.question:
Read the following passage and answer the question.

"If you are looking for a history of India, or for an account of the rise and fall of the British Raj, or for all reason of the cleaving of the subcontinent into two mutually antagonistic parts and the effects this mutilation will have in the respective sections, and ultimately on Asia, you will not find it in these pages; for though I have spent a lifetime in the country, I lived too near the seat of events, and was too intimately associated with the actors, to get the perspective needed for impartial recording of these matters."

Which of the following statements best reflects the author's opinion?

A. An intimate association does not allow for the necessary perspective.
B. Matters are recorded with an impartial perspective.
C. An intimate association offers an impartial perspective.
D. Actors are typically associated with the impartial recording of matters.
questions.answer: A. An intimate association does not allow for the necessary perspective.
questions.to_find: statement that best reflects the author's opinion about writing history

understand.plain: The author says they lived too close to events and were too involved with the people involved to give an unbiased account of India's partition era. This is a reflection on the limitations of insider knowledge — proximity can actually cloud objectivity. The passage is an honest admission that personal involvement prevents impartial recording.

keywords.explain: splitting or dividing — here referring to the partition of the Indian subcontinent
keywords.example: The cleaving of a piece of wood results in two separate parts; similarly the subcontinent was divided into India and Pakistan.

keywords.explain: not favouring one side; neutral and unbiased in judgement
keywords.example: A referee in a sport must be impartial, treating both teams equally without personal bias.

keywords.explain: a particular point of view or way of seeing things, especially influenced by one's position or experience
keywords.example: Someone who grew up in poverty has a different perspective on wealth than someone born into affluence.

understand.visual_alt: A passage from a first-person narrator explaining their close involvement with India's history prevents them from writing impartially about it.

given.aim: identify which option correctly captures the author's stated reason for not being able to write a history of India

terms.meaning: the centre or location where important events unfolded — the author was physically and professionally close to the action
terms.example: Being at the seat of events means witnessing history firsthand, but that proximity can cloud one's judgement.
terms.connects: explains why the author lacks the distance needed for objectivity

terms.term: too intimately associated with the actors
terms.meaning: the author personally knew the key players and was deeply involved with them
terms.example: A journalist who is close friends with a politician struggles to write critically about them.
terms.connects: the core reason the author cannot record matters impartially

terms.meaning: the two hostile, opposing sections (India and Pakistan after partition) that were created from the subcontinent
terms.example: After partition, the two sides held deep political and cultural hostility toward each other.
terms.connects: the historical event the author feels unable to document without bias

given.plan: Read each option against the passage. The author explicitly says personal closeness (living near events, intimate association) prevented them from gaining the perspective needed for impartial recording. Option A captures this exactly; B, C, and D contradict or distort the passage.

steps.title: Locate the author's core claim
steps.apply: The author states: 'I lived too near the seat of events, and was too intimately associated with the actors, to get the perspective needed for impartial recording.' This directly says close association prevents the needed perspective.
steps.note: The author is not saying association helps — they say it hinders impartial recording.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A: 'intimate association does not allow for the necessary perspective' — matches the passage exactly. B: 'Matters are recorded with an impartial perspective' — contradicts the passage (author says they cannot record impartially). C: 'intimate association offers an impartial perspective' — directly contradicts the passage. D: 'Actors are typically associated with impartial recording' — not stated and contradicts the passage.
steps.note: Only option A correctly reflects the author's self-stated limitation.

solution.result: A. An intimate association does not allow for the necessary perspective.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal comprehension question requiring inference from passage text; no mathematical formula is needed.

questions.question:
Read the following passage and answer the question.

"Here, throughout the early 1920s, Stuart continued to fight his losing battle to allow his sepoys to wear their caste-marks and their own choice of facial hair on parade, being again reprimanded by the commander-in-chief. His retort that 'A stronger instance than this of European prejudice with relation to this country has never come under my observations' had no effect on his superiors."

Which of the following statements is most accurate based on the above paragraph?

A. Stuart's commander-in-chief was moved by this demonstration of his prejudice.
B. The Europeans were accommodating of the sepoy's desire to wear their caste-marks.
C. Stuart's losing battle refers to his inability to succeed in enabling sepoys to wear caste-marks.
D. The commander-in-chief was exempt from the European prejudice that dictated how the sepoys were to dress.
questions.answer: C. Stuart's losing battle refers to his inability to succeed in enabling sepoys to wear caste-marks.
questions.to_find: the most accurate statement inferred from the passage about Stuart's losing battle

understand.plain: Stuart was a British officer who fought for the right of his Indian sepoys (soldiers) to wear traditional caste-marks and choose their own facial hair while on parade. His commander-in-chief repeatedly reprimanded him for this stance. Despite his strong protest that this was European prejudice at work, his superiors paid no attention. The 'losing battle' refers directly to his failed effort to secure this cultural freedom for the sepoys.

keywords.explain: Indian soldiers serving in a European (especially British) colonial army
keywords.example: During the British Raj, sepoys formed the bulk of the Indian Army under British command.

keywords.explain: religious or caste-identifying marks worn on the forehead or body by Hindus and some other communities
keywords.example: A Brahmin might wear a tilak on the forehead as a caste-mark during religious or cultural observance.

keywords.explain: formally criticised or scolded by a superior authority
keywords.example: The officer was reprimanded for disobeying orders, meaning he received an official rebuke from his superiors.

understand.visual_alt: A passage describing Stuart's repeated and unsuccessful attempts to defend the cultural rights of Indian sepoys against British military orders.

given.aim: identify which option accurately describes what Stuart's 'losing battle' refers to in the passage

terms.meaning: an effort that consistently fails despite repeated attempts; a struggle with no prospect of success
terms.example: Arguing against a unanimous board decision is a losing battle — you keep trying but cannot win.
terms.connects: describes Stuart's repeated failure to win permission for the sepoys to wear their caste-marks on parade

terms.term: had no effect on his superiors
terms.meaning: Stuart's protest — even a strongly worded one calling out European prejudice — was ignored by those above him
terms.example: Writing a complaint that is filed and forgotten means your words had no effect on the decision-makers.
terms.connects: confirms that Stuart's campaign failed and his superiors were unmoved

terms.meaning: Stuart's term for the bias of European commanders who imposed their cultural norms (facial hair, uniform standards) on Indian sepoys
terms.example: Requiring everyone to follow a single cultural dress code regardless of personal or religious background reflects cultural prejudice.
terms.connects: the reason Stuart gave for why the rules were unjust — but this argument went unheeded

given.plan: The phrase 'losing battle' in context clearly refers to Stuart's repeated failed attempts to get the sepoys' cultural practices (caste-marks and choice of facial hair) accepted on parade. Match each option to the passage to find the one that is accurate.

steps.title: Identify what 'losing battle' refers to
steps.apply: The passage says Stuart 'continued to fight his losing battle to allow his sepoys to wear their caste-marks and their own choice of facial hair on parade.' The battle = his campaign to allow these practices. It is losing because he was being 'reprimanded' and his retort 'had no effect on his superiors.'
steps.note: 'Losing' here means unsuccessful, not cowardly — he kept trying despite repeated failure.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A: 'commander-in-chief was moved' — contradicted by 'had no effect on his superiors.' B: 'Europeans were accommodating' — directly contradicted; he was reprimanded. C: 'losing battle refers to inability to succeed in enabling sepoys to wear caste-marks' — matches the passage perfectly. D: 'commander-in-chief was exempt from European prejudice' — contradicted; Stuart said this very instance was European prejudice.
steps.note: Option C precisely restates the 'losing battle' phrase using the passage's own explanation.

solution.result: C. Stuart's losing battle refers to his inability to succeed in enabling sepoys to wear caste-marks.

questions.formula_note: This is a reading comprehension question; the answer is found by carefully matching the passage text to the options without any calculation.

questions.question:
Read the following passage and answer the question.

"If you are looking for a history of India, or for an account of the rise and fall of the British Raj, or for the reason of the cleaving of the subcontinent into two mutually antagonistic parts and the effects this mutilation will have in the respective sections, and ultimately on Asia, you will not find it in these pages; for though I have spent a lifetime in the country, I lived too near the seat of events, and was too intimately associated with the actors, to get the perspective needed for impartial recording of these matters."

Which of the following is closest in meaning to the word 'cleaving' as used in the passage?

A. deteriorating
B. arguing
C. departing
D. splitting
questions.to_find: the word closest in meaning to 'cleaving' in the context of the passage

understand.plain: The word 'cleaving' appears in the phrase 'the cleaving of the subcontinent into two mutually antagonistic parts.' In this context it describes the act of dividing or splitting the Indian subcontinent (into India and Pakistan at partition). The question tests vocabulary in context — 'cleave' has two opposite meanings in English (to split apart, or to cling to), but the context here makes 'splitting' unambiguous.

keywords.explain: the act of splitting or dividing something, especially forcefully — here the partition of the subcontinent into two separate nations
keywords.example: A cleaver splits meat cleanly in two; similarly the subcontinent was cleaved into India and Pakistan in 1947.

keywords.explain: hostile toward each other; the two parts having opposing interests and hostility
keywords.example: After a bitter separation, two rival companies may become mutually antagonistic, working against each other's interests.

keywords.explain: the act of damaging or disfiguring something severely — the author uses it as a metaphor for the harm done by partition
keywords.example: Cutting a painting in half would be a mutilation of the artwork; the author sees partition as mutilation of the subcontinent.

understand.visual_alt: A vocabulary-in-context question asking for the meaning of 'cleaving' (splitting) in a passage about India's partition.

given.aim: determine the word among the choices that best replaces 'cleaving' while preserving the sentence's meaning

terms.meaning: the phrase following 'cleaving' confirms the meaning: something is being divided into two hostile halves
terms.example: The clause 'into two parts' works as a direct clue — something is being split in two.
terms.connects: narrows the meaning of 'cleaving' to splitting or dividing, not any of the other distractors

terms.meaning: becoming worse in quality or condition — does not imply division into two parts
terms.example: A building deteriorates over years of neglect, but this does not mean it splits into two pieces.
terms.connects: ruled out because deteriorating does not imply producing two separate parts

terms.meaning: dividing into two or more parts — directly equivalent to 'cleaving' in this context
terms.example: Splitting a log of wood into two halves is a classic example of cleaving.
terms.connects: the correct synonym that fits the passage context exactly

given.plan: Read the sentence substituting each option. Only 'splitting' preserves the meaning that the subcontinent was divided into two hostile parts. 'Deteriorating', 'arguing', and 'departing' do not logically complete the phrase 'cleaving of the subcontinent into two parts.'

steps.title: Use contextual clues to determine the meaning
steps.apply: The sentence reads: 'the cleaving of the subcontinent into two mutually antagonistic parts.' The key phrase 'into two...parts' tells us something is being divided. 'Cleave' (from Old English 'cleofan') means to split or sever. Substituting: 'the splitting of the subcontinent into two...parts' — this works perfectly and is historically accurate (India-Pakistan partition).
steps.note: Note that 'cleave' also has a secondary meaning (to cling/adhere), but the phrase 'into two parts' makes the splitting meaning unambiguous here.

steps.title: Eliminate other options by substitution
steps.apply: A. 'deteriorating of the subcontinent into two parts' — nonsensical; deterioration does not produce parts. B. 'arguing of the subcontinent into two parts' — grammatically and semantically wrong. C. 'departing of the subcontinent into two parts' — departing means leaving, not dividing. D. 'splitting of the subcontinent into two parts' — perfectly coherent and historically accurate.
steps.note: The substitution test immediately eliminates A, B, and C.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary-in-context question; the answer relies on word meaning and passage context rather than any formula.
